Task
NORTH AMERICA (NA)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), Esker, CSV (OpenText), EDI
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker) / Manual (exceptions, new SKUs, data sync
failures)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order with order number generated in SAP
Process Note:
• "EDI & Esker are dominant for order intake (Canada & US domestic orders)"
• "OpenText is heavily used for automated entry (Export orders -- JKSEAT region)"
• "Manual entry and intervention are required for exceptions, new SKUs, or data sync
failures"
• "For export manual changes are required on specific scenarios ex. Same sold to/ship to and
different incoterms depending on loading WH, when samples are loading along with a full
container load"
• "CSV file transfers are unique to NA, used by Canada domestic for some samples and by
JKSEAT region for customer orders"
Key Comment: "Manual entry and intervention are required for exceptions, new SKUs, or data
sync failures. For export manual changes are required on specific scenarios ex. Same sold to/
ship to and different incoterms depending on loading WH, when samples are loading along with
a full container load."
Unique Aspect: CSV file processing unique to North America; OpenText boundary system
for export orders; Frequent manual review due to diversity of intake channels and customer
changes
SAP Migration Consideration: VA01 usage already established; Need to ensure Esker and
OpenText integration validated in S/4HANA; CSV file handling process must be maintained
 
MEXICO
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service, QSR, Retail), SAP VA01 (Assumption), Edicom/EDI
(Retail), Gmail (Food service)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Food service via Thalamus) / Automated (EDI for retail)
McCain    5|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System order generated by platform (Thalamus/EDI/SAP)
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is the main platform for food service and QSR; Edicom EDI is used for retail"
• "Manual entry is common, especially for food service; EDI is automated for retail"
• "Heavy reliance on Gmail for food service notifications and customer communication"
• "Order acknowledgment can be automated or manual, and may be requested by the
customer"
• "Common check points: price, route, warehouse, weight, duplicate order, requested delivery
date, etc."
Key Comment: "Heavy reliance on Gmail for food service notifications and customer
communication"
Unique Aspect: Gmail extensively used for food service operational communications;
Thalamus serves multiple channels (Food service, QSR, Retail); Edicom EDI specific to retail
channel
SAP Migration Consideration: Thalamus and Edicom EDI integrations must be validated;
Gmail-based workflow formalization needed; VA01 standard transaction confirmed
 
CALATIN (Central America & Caribbean)
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service), SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI (Retail), Day & Ross (DRTN
for NA-sourced)
Manual/Automated: Manual (frequent) / Automated (EDI for retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record in SAP
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is used for food service; SAP for system entry"
• "Manual entry is frequent, especially for exports and smaller segments"
• "Exports require extra documentation and compliance checks"
• "NA-sourced orders may integrate with Day & Ross (DRTN)"
• "Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with full traceability for export
compliance"
Key Comment: "Exports require extra documentation and compliance checks" and "NA-sourced
orders may integrate with Day & Ross (DRTN)"
Unique Aspect: Export-focused operations with enhanced documentation requirements; Day
& Ross (DRTN) integration for NA-sourced orders; Thalamus for food service segment
SAP Migration Consideration: Export documentation workflows must be preserved; DRTN
integration for NA-sourced orders needs validation; Manual export handling processes require
clear procedures in S/4HANA
 
ANDEAN (Colombia)
System/Tool: Thalamus, Carvajal, SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI
Manual/Automated: Manual (portal handling) / Automated (EDI/Carvajal for retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record
Process Note:
• "Thalamus and Carvajal are primary intake platforms; SAP is used for system entry"
McCain    6|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Manual portal handling, especially for food service (Actualmente se esta migrando a
Thalamus)"
• "EDI/Carvajal standards apply for retail and exports"
• "Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with data retention for
compliance"
Key Comment: "Manual portal handling, especially for food service.(Actualmente se esta
migrando a Thalamus)"
Unique Aspect: Carvajal platform specific to Andean region for retail; Migration to Thalamus
in progress; EDI/Carvajal standards for retail and exports
SAP Migration Consideration: Thalamus migration ongoing - ensure smooth transition;
Carvajal integration must be validated in S/4HANA; Data retention for compliance mandatory
 
BRAZIL
System/Tool: SAP Fiori (Primary), SAP ECC VA01 (Exceptions: CPU, McD, BK, maritime,
donations/samples), EDI, Neo Grid (Retail)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Fiori entries) / Automated (EDI, Neo Grid)
Performer: CSR (Fiori) / SALES ADM Team (SAP ECC exceptions)
Output: SAP order generated in Fiori or ECC depending on scenario
Process Note:
• "SAP Fiori is the main portal for domestic and CPU orders; SAP ECC VA01 is used for special
cases (CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples)"
• "SALES ADM team manages SAP ECC entries for exceptions and special orders"
• "WhatsApp/email are common for customer communication and error resolution"
• "Proforma invoices for CPU orders are issued via SAP ECC VF01/VF02"
• "Strong SAP Fiori adoption; exceptions handled manually in ECC"
Key Comment: "SAP Fiori is the main portal for domestic and CPU orders; SAP ECC VA01 is used
for special cases (CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples)" and "SALES ADM team manages
SAP ECC entries for exceptions and special orders"
Unique Aspect: Dual system architecture (Fiori primary + ECC exceptions); SALES ADM team
handles special scenarios; Strong Fiori adoption among all LATAM regions; WhatsApp business
communication; Proforma invoice generation (VF01/VF02)
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Dual system (Fiori + ECC) needs consolidation
strategy in S/4HANA; Special handling for CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples must be
accommodated; SALES ADM team workflows require clear definition; Proforma invoice process
(VF01/VF02) validation
 
URUGUAY
System/Tool: Thalamus, Power Street, EDI, SAP VA01 (Assumption)
Manual/Automated: Manual (domestic via Thalamus/Power Street) / Automated (EDI retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record
Process Note:
• "EDI, Thalamus, and Power Street are used for order intake; Power Street is unique to
Uruguay"
• "Manual entry is common for domestic orders; EDI for retail"
• "WhatsApp and email are standard for customer communication and error resolution"
McCain    7|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Power Street: Tool to input customer orders for direct and indirect sales. This is used to
load orders on every truck in Uruguay. In this country, sales is a little different from the rest
of McCain. Orders are loaded on the spot (trucks)."
Key Comment: "Power Street is unique to Uruguay" and "This is used to load orders on every
truck in Uruguay. In this country, sales is a little different from the rest of McCain. Orders are
loaded on the spot (trucks)."
Unique Aspect: Power Street ONLY exists in Uruguay; On-the-spot truck loading model;
Unique sales operational model different from rest of McCain; Direct and indirect sales via
mobile tool
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Power Street unique to Uruguay must be integrated
or replaced in S/4HANA; Mobile truck loading workflow is business-critical; Operational model
significantly different from other regions
 
ARGENTINA
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service), Krikos360/EDI (Retail), Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone (QSR),
SAP VA01 (Assumption)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Food service, QSR) / Automated (EDI retail via Krikos360)
Performer: CSR / Operations (varies by channel)
Output: System order by platform (Thalamus/Krikos360/SAP)
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is used for food service; Krikos360/EDI for retail; Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone for
QSR"
• "Manual entry is standard for food service and QSR; EDI for retail"
• "WhatsApp and Gmail are common for rapid customer communication"
• "Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy; EDI for retail"
Key Comment: "WhatsApp and Gmail are common for rapid customer communication" and
"Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy"
Unique Aspect: Krikos360 platform (Planexware) specific to Argentina retail; Multi-channel
digital communication (Gmail, WhatsApp, Phone) for QSR; Channel-specific tool strategy
SAP Migration Consideration: Krikos360/Planexware integration validation critical; WhatsApp/
Gmail workflow formalization needed; Multi-channel tool landscape requires consolidation
assessment
 
APAC-ANZ (Australia & New Zealand)
System/Tool: Portalink, EDI, Email, SAP VA02/VA03 (NOT VA01 for EDI)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Portalink) / Manual (email-based changes)
Performer: Customer Solutions Team
Output: SAP sales order
Process Note:
• "EDI orders are handled automatically in SAP . All changes to customer orders, regardless of
how the original order was received, are processed manually in SAP based on email"
• "Portalink Integration: Serves as critical boundary system converting non-EDI orders into
SAP-compatible format, enabling immediate automated acknowledgements for non-EDI
customers"
• "ANZ: VA01 is not used for EDI order entry. VA02 and VA03 is used"
• "HighRadius Financial Integration: Used for credit checks and payment tracking"
• "Excel Macro Automation: End-of-day order confirmations automated through Excel
macros"
McCain    8|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Z7 CSR Hold Implementation: Universal application of Z7 CSR hold enables manual review
and allocation rule enforcement by commercial team"
Key Comment: "EDI orders are handled automatically in SAP . All changes to customer orders,
regardless of how the original order was received, are processed manually in SAP based on
email" and "VA01 is not used for EDI order entry. VA02 and VA03 is used"
Unique Aspect: CRITICAL: VA01 NOT used for EDI order entry - ANZ uses VA02/VA03;
Portalink boundary system converts non-EDI to SAP format; HighRadius for credit/payment;
Excel macro for end-of-day confirmations; Z7 CSR Hold universal; Duplicate EDI order manual
deletion required
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - ANZ does NOT use VA01 for EDI orders (uses VA02/
VA03) - This is UNIQUE and must be carefully evaluated in S/4HANA migration; Portalink
boundary system integration critical; HighRadius integration validation; Excel macro process
may need modernization; Z7 CSR Hold implementation; ATP functionality gap (currently no ATP)
 
APAC-CHINA
System/Tool: Digital Hub (Cloud-based portal - MANDATORY for ALL orders), SAP VA01 (via
EDI integration from Digital Hub)
Manual/Automated: Manual entry into Digital Hub / Automated (Digital Hub to SAP via EDI)
Performer: Customer Service Team
Output: SAP sales order triggered via EDI from Digital Hub after validation
Process Note:
• "All customer purchase orders (retail or food service channels) are received and processed
through the cloud-based Digital Hub portal"
• "Orders are manually entered into the Digital Hub, where validations and changes are
performed before triggering sales orders into SAP via EDI integration"
• "No Direct Customer EDI: All orders flow through Digital Hub regardless of customer
technical capability"
• "Real-time Integration: Direct integration with warehouse management systems provides
accurate, current inventory data for ATP decisions"
• "Auto-cancellation Logic: Sophisticated cancellation workflows with reason codes (Y1, Y5) for
unpaid orders enable automated revenue protection"
• "ZF delivery blocks preferred over credit blocks for payment terms enforcement"
Key Comment: "All customer purchase orders (retail or food service channels) are received and
processed through the cloud-based Digital Hub portal" and "Orders are manually entered into
the Digital Hub, where validations and changes are performed before triggering sales orders
into SAP via EDI integration"
Unique Aspect: Digital Hub MANDATORY for 100% of orders regardless of channel; No
direct customer-to-SAP EDI; Dual ATP (Digital Hub "light" + SAP "final"); ZF delivery block for
prepayment instead of credit blocks; Auto-cancel Y5 rejection code for unpaid prepay orders;
WMS real-time integration; Channel-specific CRM (Food Service) vs email (Retail/QSR)
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Digital Hub is mandatory gateway (no direct SAP
access); Digital Hub to S/4HANA EDI integration must be validated; Dual ATP mechanism must
be maintained; ZF delivery block logic; Auto-cancellation workflows (Y1, Y5); WMS integration
validation; Prepayment enforcement logic
 
APAC-INDIA
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI via OpenText (Reliance ONLY), Email, Spreadsheet,
Bheem DMS
McCain    9|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI for select modern trade) / Manual (DC staff entry for non-
EDI)
Performer: DC Executives / Customer Service Team
Output: SAP sales order
Process Note:
• "EDI orders from select customers in modern trade (e.g.,: Reliance) are automatically
integrated into SAP via OpenText, while non-EDI orders received by email or spreadsheet are
manually entered by distribution center staff"
• "DC-based Processing: Decentralized order entry model contrasts with other regions'
centralized approaches"
• "Minimal Automation: Limited automated order acknowledgements (only Reliance) reflects
low digitalization maturity"
• "Reliance Special Treatment: Only customer receiving automated order acknowledgements,
indicating strategic partnership requirements"
• "Manual-first Philosophy: Preference for manual processes over automation, possibly due
to complexity and control requirements"
• "MB5T used for stock in transit checks, crucial for multi-location inventory management"
Key Comment: "EDI orders from select customers in modern trade (e.g.,: Reliance) are
automatically integrated into SAP via OpenText, while non-EDI orders received by email or
spreadsheet are manually entered by distribution center staff" and "Only customer receiving
automated order acknowledgements (Reliance)"
Unique Aspect: Decentralized DC-based order entry model; ONLY Reliance has EDI and
automated acknowledgement; Manual-first philosophy; OpenText for select modern
trade EDI; Export orders processed in monthly cycles; MB5T for stock in transit checks
(multi-location); Bheem DMS for retail claims; 100% credit block by default; FSSAI license
validation at order creation; TDP days (10/20/30) affect transport capacity
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Decentralized DC-based entry model unique to India;
OpenText integration for Reliance EDI; Manual entry by DC staff workflows; Minimal automation
- high manual dependency; MB5T stock in transit functionality; FSSAI license validation must be
maintained; Default credit block process; TDP days operational logic
 
APAC-JKSEAT (Japan, Korea, SE Asia & Taiwan)
System/Tool:
• Japan: SAP VA01/ME21 (Assumption), Fax, Customer websites, EDI, CSV macro
• KSEAT: SAP VA01 (Assumption), CSV files, Macro/EDI watcher, Email
Manual/Automated: Manual (Fax, websites in Japan) / Automated (CSV macro, EDI for both)
Performer: Customer Service Team
Output: SAP sales order, Proforma invoice (as order confirmation)
Process Note:
• Japan: "Customer orders are captured through multiple channels: fax, customer websites,
EDI, and CSV files. Fax and website orders are manually entered into SAP , while EDI and CSV
file orders are automatically integrated into SAP"
• KSEAT: "Orders are received via email as either CSV files or manual requests. CSV files are
uploaded to a shared folder, then picked up by EDI or a macro for automatic entry into SAP ,
while manual orders are entered directly into SAP"
• "Traditional Communication Channels: Japan maintains fax communication alongside
modern channels, reflecting local business culture and customer preferences"
McCain    10|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Advanced Order Planning: Orders placed 6 weeks to 4 months in advance eliminates need
for real-time inventory visibility but requires sophisticated planning"
• "CSV Automation: KSEAT uses shared folder mechanism with macro/EDI pickup for CSV
automation"
• "Proforma Invoice as confirmation: KC sends Proforma Invoice (manual PDF from SAP) as
order confirmation; HAVI/McDonald's excluded"
• "No local ATP/MOQ gating at JKC; orders are taken weeks to months ahead (min ~6 weeks),
and sourcing regions (e.g., NA, EU) run ATP on their side"
Key Comment: "Japan: Customer orders are captured through multiple channels: fax, customer
websites, EDI, and CSV files" and "Orders placed 6 weeks to 4 months in advance" and "No local
ATP/MOQ gating at JKC"
Unique Aspect: Traditional fax still used in Japan; CSV macro/shared folder automation
in KSEAT; Proforma invoice used as order confirmation (not standard acknowledgement);
No local ATP - sourcing regions handle ATP; Long lead times (6 weeks to 4 months); ME21 for
purchase orders alongside VA01; Power BI for shipment tracking; Regional orders 6-16 weeks
ahead of ship date
SAP Migration Consideration: Japan VA01/ME21 usage must be validated; Fax integration/
workflow documentation needed; CSV macro automation from shared folders; Proforma invoice
generation process; No local ATP - sourcing region dependency; Long planning horizon (6
weeks-4 months) must be supported; Power BI integration for tracking
 
GREAT BRITAIN (GB)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption for order creation), EDI, Esker, Email (Manual), ZV05N
(for display)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker) / Manual (email orders)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: Manually entered order (from email) / Automated order from EDI/Esker
Process Note:
• "GB receives orders through three primary channels: EDI (automated), email (manual entry
required), and Esker (PDF document processing)"
• "Transaction codes used: VA01 for order creation, ZV05N for displaying orders, VA02 for
order amendments"
• "EDI orders undergo only basic sense checks; no comprehensive price validation at entry"
• "if there is an error in the EDI it will generate a failed idoc which can be viewed in WE05"
• "Incompletion log in SAP flags missing data, causing EDI orders to fail and go on hold,
equally if the PO is a duplicate SAP puts the order on hold"
• "Manual orders similarly have no price check at entry; incompletion log identifies gaps"
• "Manual process only - no automatic customer notification upon order entry"
• "All orders entered using t-code VA01, where the order type is specified"
Key Comment: "GB receives orders through three primary channels: EDI (automated), email
(manual entry required), and Esker (PDF document processing)" and "Manual process only - no
automatic customer notification upon order entry" and "All orders entered using t-code VA01,
where the order type is specified"
Unique Aspect: ZV05N used for displaying orders (alongside VA03); WE05 for failed IDoc
monitoring; Esker reads PDF documents from customers; No automatic acknowledgement
- manual email confirmation only; No price validation at entry - handled later as claims via
AR inbox; Incompletion log flags missing data; Duplicate PO auto-hold; EDI MDN available on
customer request
SAP Migration Consideration: VA01 standard usage confirmed; ZV05N display transaction
validation in S/4HANA; WE05 IDoc monitoring; Esker PDF integration; Manual acknowledgement
McCain    11|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
process; No price validation at entry - pricing claims handled downstream; Incompletion log
dependency
 
EMENA (Europe, Middle East, North Africa)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), ZVA05N (import transaction), EDI, Esker, CSV process
(Italy), Manual (email/phone)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker, CSV Italy) / Manual (email/phone)
Performer: Customer Service Representative / System (for off-time import)
Output: Orders imported into SAP
Process Note:
• "Order requests could be received via various platforms: Manual entry (email & phone call),
ESKER (reads PDF documents from customers), EDI, CSV process (Italy) via EDI"
• "Orders from various channels are imported into SAP system during off time"
• "Only applicable to EMENA"
• T-codes mentioned: "VA01, VA02, ZVA05N, ZG_MTD_SHIPMENT, VL01N, VL02N, VL06"
Key Comment: "Orders from various channels are imported into SAP system during off time"
and "Only applicable to EMENA"
Unique Aspect: ZVA05N import transaction ONLY in EMENA; Off-time import process
unique to this region; CSV process specific to Italy via EDI; Esker PDF document processing;
Multi-channel intake (EDI, Esker, CSV, Manual); Import batch processing during off-peak hours
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - ZVA05N off-time import transaction unique to
EMENA must be evaluated for S/4HANA equivalent; Italy CSV process via EDI; Esker integration;
Batch import scheduling and timing; Multi-channel consolidation during off-time import
 
SOUTH AFRICA (SA)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), Email (Direct), EDI via Vector (Indirect 3PL)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Direct Network) / Automated (Indirect Network via Vector EDI)
Performer: Customer Service Representative (Direct) / Vector System (Indirect)
Output: Manually entered order (Direct) / EDI order via Vector (Indirect)
Process Note:
• "Purchase requests received via email" (Direct Network)
• "Direct Network: Orders received manually by delivery network"
• "Indirect Network (3PLs/Vector): EDI captures orders and sends to Vector directly from
customers; next day, orders communicated to McCain for backward integration into SAP
(boundary system), operating on a consignment model"
• "(Direct) Customer Service receives purchase requests via email or manual channels and
manually enters into SAP . T-code VA01 used for order creation"
• "(Indirect) EDI captures orders and sends to Vector directly from customers"
• "Proforma invoice/manual order confirmation for some customers"
Key Comment: "Direct Network: Orders received manually by delivery network" and "Indirect
Network (3PLs/Vector): EDI captures orders and sends to Vector directly from customers; next
day, orders communicated to McCain for backward integration into SAP (boundary system),
operating on a consignment model"
Unique Aspect: Dual network model - Direct vs Indirect (3PL/Vector); Vector 3PL ONLY in
South Africa; Consignment model for indirect network; Next-day backward integration to
SAP; Proforma invoice for some customers; T-code VA01 confirmed for order creation; Manual
entry for direct network
McCain    12|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Dual network model (Direct manual vs Indirect
Vector EDI); Vector 3PL boundary system integration critical; Consignment model logic; Next-day
backward integration timing and process; Proforma invoice generation for specific customers;
VA01 standard usage
(as-is) IT System: SAP , Digital Hub, Excel(CSV), Thalamus
IT Systems:
- SAP ECC
SAP/SSC/Executable: SAP - VA01, VA02
Organization units:  01.05.01.03 Enter Order Into System - Customer Solutions/3PL
Dictionary entries: SAP ECC
Task type: Receive
Enter Consignment Details into the ERP
Task
NORTH AMERICA (NA)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), Esker, CSV (OpenText), EDI
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker) / Manual (exceptions, new SKUs, data sync
failures)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order with order number generated in SAP

the same business in real time.

SAP ECC (Enterprise Central Component)
SAP ECC (ERP Central Component) is a comprehensive enterprise resource planning (ERP)
software suite developed by SAP . It serves as the core component of many organizations’ IT
landscapes, integrating key business processes and data across departments, and enabling
efficient management of resources, operations, and reporting.Key 
Features of SAP ECC:Centralized database for seamless data flow across business functions
• Modular structure, allowing organizations to implement only the modules they need
• Real-time processing and reporting capabilities
• Strong integration with other SAP and third-party systems
SAP CO, a module within SAP ECC that focuses on internal accounting and management
accounting, providing tools for planning, controlling, and analyzing costs and revenues. It is tightly
integrated with SAP FI (Financial Accounting) and is often referred to as SAP FICO. SAP CO helps
businesses track costs associated with various business processes, allocate costs, and manage
profitability analysis.

refined_blueprint.strategy_report_markdown:
# Re-Imagined Strategy Report: Order Intake

## 1. Executive Summary — One Big Move

The One Big Move is to deploy a Clean Core-compliant Order Intake Side-Car as a System of Intelligence that orchestrates multi-channel intake, deterministic rules, and agentic perception across regions, while leaving the ERP kernel unchanged. This directly targets global manual re-keying from email/PDF/fax/spreadsheets (F-029, [DOC1], [DOC2]), EDI failure triage (F-030, [DOC1]), mandatory China Digital Hub handoffs (F-033, [DOC1]), and regional manual holds/acknowledgements (F-014, F-023) by absorbing logic and automation in the Side-Car. In the current Trust Gap Phase: Shadow, the Side-Car runs in parallel to human CSR workflows and SAP transactions (VA01/VA02, per evidence), learning from friction patterns and proving precision against path decisions (A/B/C). This move reduces cycle time and errors documented across regions (e.g., NA, Mexico, EMENA, APAC-ANZ/Japan/India), standardizes gateway decisions (F-032), and ensures all custom logic resides outside the ERP kernel, enforcing Clean Core.

## 2. Cognitive Friction Analysis — The friction table (reproduce as-is)

## 4. Architecture of the Future State — Hub-and-spoke operating model with agent personas

- Operating Model
  - Hub: Order Intake Side-Car (System of Intelligence)
    - Purpose: Centralize intake, classification, validation, and orchestration for multi-channel orders while preserving ERP Clean Core. Anchored by evidence of global manual intake across email/PDF/fax/spreadsheets (F-029, [DOC1], [DOC2]) and region-specific manual patterns (F-003 Mexico Gmail; F-008 Brazil WhatsApp; F-019 Japan fax/web; F-022 GB email; F-024 EMENA email/phone).
    - Scope: Intake adapters, perception (OCR/NLP) for unstructured channels, deterministic rule services for export/incoterms and holds, exception triage for EDI failures (F-030, [DOC1]), gateway compliance (F-033 Digital Hub).
    - Clean Core Enforcement: All custom logic resides in the Side-Car. The ERP kernel is not modified; interactions are via standard APIs/IDoc interfaces referenced in [DOC1] (EDI/IDoc failure mentions) and SAP transactions (VA01/VA02/VA03) evidenced in multiple friction items.
  - Spokes: Regional channel connectors
    - NA: EDI/Esker/CSV adapters to reduce manual exceptions (F-001).
    - Mexico: Gmail adapter for food service notifications (F-003), acknowledgement workflow (F-004).
    - CALATIN/ANDEAN: Portal adapters (Thalamus, Carvajal) for food service and export corrections (F-005, F-006).
    - Brazil: WhatsApp and email connectors (F-008), Fiori/ECC routing rationalization for special cases (F-007).
    - Uruguay: Power Street mobile intake normalization (F-009, F-034).
    - Argentina: Multichannel intake (Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone, Krikos360/EDI) (F-010, F-011).
    - APAC-ANZ: EDI monitoring, manual change capture, duplicate detection (F-012, F-013, F-014).
    - APAC-China: Digital Hub integration (F-015, F-033).
    - APAC-India: Email/spreadsheet entry normalization (F-016), acknowledgement workflow (F-017), MB5T check scheduling (F-018).
    - APAC-Japan/KSEAT: Fax OCR, website scraping, CSV watcher (F-019, F-020), proforma generation routing (F-021).
    - GB: Email/Esker/EDI intake (F-022), acknowledgement automation (F-023).
    - EMENA: Email/phone intake reduction (F-024).
    - South Africa: Email direct network intake; Vector EDI recon (F-025, F-035).

- Agent Personas (embedded in Side-Car, not ERP)
  - Intake Scribe: Perceives and transcribes multi-channel orders into structured payloads (supports F-003, F-008, F-010, F-019, F-022, F-024, F-025, F-029).
  - Intent Analyzer: Classifies order-type (standard vs consignment) and determines required rule-based adjustments (supports F-002, F-032, Z7 hold context in F-014).
  - Dispute Judge: Reconciles deductions/disputes across evidence systems (supports F-036).

- Governance and Control Points
  - Decision Gate Standardization: Remove inconsistent manual branching (F-028) by encoding gateway criteria in Side-Car, executed before posting to ERP (Clean Core).
  - Exception Handling: EDI and CSV exceptions processed in Side-Car (F-030, F-020) with alerting and retry, reducing manual triage.
  - Regional Gateway Compliance: Respect China Digital Hub mandate (F-033) and local nuances (regional JSON registry below) while centralizing orchestration.

## 5. Technical Stack — System of Intelligence, System of Record, integration contracts

- System of Intelligence (Side-Car)
  - Channel Adapters: Email (Gmail), WhatsApp, Fax OCR, PDF parsing, CSV watcher for shared folders (KSEAT, F-020), portal connectors (Thalamus, Carvajal), mobile Power Street adapter (Uruguay, F-009/F-034).
  - Perception Services: OCR/NLP for unstructured sources (aligned with F-029, F-019, F-022, F-024).
  - Rule Services: Export/incoterms enforcement (F-002), Z7 hold release criteria (F-014), order-type classification (F-032), deduplication for EDI (F-013), Digital Hub pass-through logic (F-033), acknowledgement workflows (F-004, F-017, F-023).
  - Exception Orchestrator: EDI failure triage (IDoc failure evidence in [DOC1], F-030), CSV pickup failure monitoring (F-020), vector reconciliation scheduling (F-035).
  - Audit and Traceability: Conversation capture from WhatsApp/email (F-008), evidence ledger for disputes (F-036). All maintained in Side-Car, linked to ERP order references.

- System of Record
  - ERP: SAP ECC/ERP (VA01/VA02/VA03 referenced across [DOC1]/[DOC2]).
  - Integration Contracts: Standard IDoc-based EDI processing (referenced in [DOC1] via “EDI failure” and IDoc context), outbound acknowledgements via ERP outputs (F-004, F-017, F-023), no ERP kernel modifications. All posting/changes executed through published APIs/IDoc interfaces to maintain Clean Core.

- Integration Principles (Clean Core)
  - No custom code in ERP kernel. Side-Car performs all custom logic, sends validated, fully classified payloads to ERP.
  - ERP remains authoritative for order record; Side-Car maintains intake provenance, evidence links, and channel artifacts.

## 6. Integration Design Deep Dive — Staged processing contract (intake, classification, ERP posting)

- Stage 1: Intake
  - Trigger Channels: Email, PDF, Fax, Spreadsheet (global, F-029); WhatsApp (Brazil, Argentina, F-008/F-010); Portals (Thalamus/Carvajal/Krikos360, F-003/F-006/F-011/F-010); CSV shared folder (KSEAT, F-020); Mobile (Power Street, Uruguay, F-009/F-034); Digital Hub (China, F-015/F-033).
  - Contract: Side-Car standard schema: Header (sold-to/ship-to, incoterms), Line Items (SKU, quantity, UoM), Delivery (date/time windows), References (PO, portal IDs), Evidence (channel message IDs, file hashes).
  - Controls: Schema validation; channel provenance capture (for F-008 traceability); duplicate detection (F-013).

- Stage 2: Classification
  - Order-Type Determination: Standard vs consignment per documented gateways (F-032, [DOC1], [DOC2]).
  - Rule-Based Adjustments: Export adjustments (incoterms, sample-with-FCL scenarios) per North America evidence (F-002); Z7 CSR hold treatment (ANZ, F-014); China mandatory Digital Hub step enforcement (F-033).
  - Exception Prediction: Identify EDI risk (F-030) and portal anomalies, route to agentic review (Intake Scribe/Intent Analyzer) during Shadow phase for human comparison.

- Stage 3: ERP Posting
  - Posting Path: Side-Car converts classified payload to ERP-compatible structure and submits via standard interfaces (evidence of EDI/IDoc context in [DOC1]).
  - Acknowledgement: Automated confirmations for regions with manual acknowledgements (Mexico, India, GB per F-004, F-017, F-023), respecting local templates and registry policies.
  - Change Management: Side-Car orchestrates VA02-like changes when sourced from email (F-012, [DOC1], [DOC2]). During Shadow, changes are suggested, with CSR finalization.
  - Reconciliation: Vector backward integration schedule (SA, F-035), MB5T check automation for India (F-018) as pre/posting availability checks coordinated outside ERP (Clean Core).

- Operational Safeguards
  - Idempotency: Prevent duplicate ERP postings (mitigate F-013).
  - Retry/Backoff: EDI/IDoc transient failures (F-030).
  - Observability: Channel-level and region-level dashboards for intake volume and exception rates, anchored to friction points.

## 7. Agent Persona Reasoning Model — Intake Scribe, Intent Analyzer, Dispute Judge boundaries

- Intake Scribe
  - Mission: Transform unstructured inputs into structured order payloads with evidence markers. Applies to email, WhatsApp, fax, phone summaries, PDFs, and spreadsheets (F-003, F-008, F-010, F-019, F-022, F-024, F-025, F-029).
  - Boundaries: Does not commit orders in ERP; prepares payloads and confidence scores. During Shadow, runs parallel to CSR manual entry; discrepancies are flagged for review.
  - Evidence Anchors: Unstructured intake documented across regions and channels ([DOC1], [DOC2], F-029; region-specific items above).

- Intent Analyzer
  - Mission: Decide order-type (standard vs consignment) and apply rule services for exports/incoterms, consignment routing, Z7 hold logic (F-002, F-032, F-014).
  - Boundaries: Executes deterministic rules in Side-Car; no kernel logic changes. During Shadow, recommendations rendered to CSR with traceability to evidence references and regional policies.

- Dispute Judge
  - Mission: Triage deductions/disputes by correlating evidence across systems (F-036, [DOC1]).
  - Boundaries: Does not alter ERP financial records; compiles reconciliation packets and recommended actions. Runs in Side-Car; ensures audit trail of evidence provenance.

- Persona Governance
  - Confidence Calibration: Leverage path decision confidence notes (e.g., 90–97%) as initial calibration references; all agent outputs in Shadow require human confirmation.
  - Escalation: When agent confidence is below path-design expectations or channel anomalies exist (e.g., CSV pickup failure per F-020), route to human CSR.

## 8. The Trust Gap Protocol — Shadow / Co-Pilot / Autopilot phases with confidence thresholds

- Shadow (Current Phase; per Process Context)
  - Description: Agents operate in parallel to human workflows; produce payloads and recommendations without committing ERP transactions. CSRs compare Side-Car outputs against their manual entries (supported by evidence of manual entries across regions, e.g., VA01/VA02/VA03 use in [DOC1]/[DOC2]).
  - Confidence Handling: Align to path decision confidences noted for each friction item (e.g., 97% for deduplication F-013 and email intake F-022 classification rationale). Agent outputs are logged with confidence and rationale; any discrepancies trigger remediation and learning.

- Co-Pilot
  - Description: Agents pre-fill ERP operation requests and propose decisions (order-type, holds, acknowledgements) while CSRs approve/override. Anchored to deterministic A-path items (e.g., F-002, F-014, F-032) and B-path items requiring perception but stable patterns (e.g., email intake segments F-022).
  - Confidence Handling: Progression from Shadow to Co-Pilot per friction pathway when observed accuracy aligns with path confidence rationales. Governance boards review empirical precision against friction evidence.

- Autopilot
  - Description: Agents commit transactions via Side-Car→ERP integration for scoped pathways, with post-commit alerts. Applies first to fully deterministic A-path flows (export/incoterms enforcement, acknowledgement automation) and then to B-path segments achieving sustained accuracy and traceability.
  - Confidence Handling: Requires sustained alignment with path decision confidence levels and regional sign-off; exception handling remains in Side-Car.

All phases enforce Clean Core—no ERP kernel changes; Side-Car handles perception, rules, and orchestration.

## 9. Path Design Decisions — Bullet list of each friction point's path assignment with rationale

- F-001 — Path B: Deterministic exception-handling and sync rules can automate these cases; no unstructured inputs require perception or complex reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-002 — Path A: Standard ERP configuration can enforce Incoterms and export rules (rule-based); no need for perception or probabilistic reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-003 — Path B: Requires perception of unstructured Gmail communications and extraction of order details; may need adaptive handling of varied email formats. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-004 — Path B: Order acknowledgements can be sent deterministically via workflow/output rules; no perception or complex reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-005 — Path A: Export and smaller-segment orders can be standardized in core ERP with rule-based fields and compliance checks; no unstructured inputs noted. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-006 — Path B: Portal intake and compliance corrections can be automated with deterministic workflows; no unstructured perception required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-007 — Path A: Enforce standard front-end (Fiori) usage and reduce ECC special cases via core process standardization; rule-based routing, no perception needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-008 — Path B: WhatsApp/email are unstructured channels requiring perception and contextual reasoning to ensure traceability and correct ERP updates. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-009 — Path B: On-the-spot truck orders can be automated via deterministic integration/workflows; no explicit need for perception or complex reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-010 — Path B: Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone inputs are unstructured and require perception and adaptive handling to reduce transcription errors and latency. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-011 — Path B: Retail/hardcopy reviews require perception of unstructured documents and judgment; fits probabilistic reasoning rather than deterministic rules. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-012 — Path B: Email-driven change requests require perception to extract intent and details and may need adaptive handling of varied change scenarios. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-013 — Path B: Duplicate EDI orders can be eliminated via deterministic deduplication rules/IDoc checks; no perception or adaptive reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-014 — Path A: Replace universal manual holds with standard ERP rule-based holds/release criteria; no need for perception or probabilistic logic. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-015 — Path B: Digital Hub intake can be automated with deterministic workflows/forms validation; no perception or complex reasoning indicated. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-016 — Path B: Email/spreadsheet inputs require perception to parse varied formats and reconcile; may need adaptive exception handling by an agent. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-017 — Path B: Order acknowledgements can be auto-generated via deterministic rules; no unstructured perception or complex reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-018 — Path B: Stock-in-transit checks (MB5T) can be scheduled/automated deterministically; no perception or probabilistic reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-019 — Path B: Fax and website orders require perception (OCR/web parsing) and adaptive handling of varied layouts; fits agentic processing. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-020 — Path B: CSV ingestion is structured and can be automated deterministically (watchers/ETL); no perception or adaptive reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-021 — Path A: Use standard ERP output determination to auto-generate/send proforma confirmations (rule-based); no perception/reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-022 — Path B: Email orders require perception of unstructured content and extraction of order data; deterministic rules alone are insufficient. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-023 — Path A: Enable standard ERP auto-notification on order entry (rule-based output); no perception or adaptive reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-024 — Path B: Email and phone inputs are unstructured, requiring perception and adaptive handling to accurately capture orders into ERP. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-025 — Path B: Email-based direct network orders require perception to extract order details; deterministic automation alone is insufficient. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-026 — Path B: Repeated manual touchpoints indicate tasks suitable for deterministic workflow/RPA; no explicit need for perception or probabilistic reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-027 — Path B: Exception branches can be routed via deterministic rules in workflows; adaptive planning not explicitly required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-028 — Path A: Standardize decision gateway criteria in core ERP to eliminate inconsistent manual branching; rule-based configuration suffices. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-029 — Path C: Email/PDF/fax/spreadsheet intake requires perception of unstructured/semi-structured content and adaptive handling of format variance. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-030 — Path B: EDI capture failures can be triaged via deterministic validation/IDoc rules; no perception or probabilistic reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-031 — Path B: Order amendments in SAP can follow deterministic workflows; perception is only needed if sourced from unstructured channels (not specified here). Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-032 — Path A: Order type classification can be enforced via standard ERP rules; deterministic, no perception or adaptive reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-033 — Path B: Mandatory gateway can be streamlined with deterministic integration/workflows; no unstructured perception or probabilistic reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-034 — Path C: Mobile Power Street payloads need adaptive normalization and exception handling; requires perception and dynamic action beyond deterministic rules. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-035 — Path B: Next-day reconciliation can be automated deterministically via integration schedules; no perception or probabilistic reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-036 — Path C: Dispute/deduction triage requires perception across multiple systems/documents and reasoning to reconcile evidence and compliance. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)

## 11. Delivery and Rollout Plan — Wave 1/2/3 with KPIs, exit criteria, and governance

- Guiding Principle: Sequence waves by Path classes and regional readiness evidenced in friction items, while maintaining Clean Core (Side-Car-only custom logic).

- Wave 1 — Deterministic Core (Path A)
  - Scope: F-002 (export/incoterms), F-005 (export/smaller segments standardization), F-007 (Brazil Fiori standardization for exceptions), F-014 (Z7 hold rule-based replacement), F-021 (proforma output determination), F-023 (auto acknowledgement).
  - Regional Touchpoints: NA exports (F-002), CALATIN exports (F-005), Brazil Fiori/ECC rationalization (F-007), ANZ holds (F-014), KSEAT proforma (F-021), GB acknowledgement (F-023).
  - KPIs (derived from friction “Why It’s Friction”):
    - Cycle time reduction for export/edit scenarios (F-002/F-005).
    - Reduction in manual acknowledgements (F-023).
    - Elimination of universal manual holds prior to allocation (F-014).
  - Exit Criteria:
    - Documented rule sets operational in Side-Car; ERP integrations via standard interfaces only (Clean Core).
    - CSR sign-off that manual edits/holds replaced by rule outcomes in Co-Pilot mode.
    - No kernel modifications in ERP.
  - Governance:
    - Region leads validate rules against local practices referenced in [DOC1].
    - Change Advisory Board confirms no core code changes; integration approvals logged.

- Wave 2 — Agentic Perception and Workflow (Path B)
  - Scope: F-001 (exceptions/new SKUs), F-003 (Mexico Gmail intake), F-004/F-017 (acknowledgements workflow automation), F-006 (portal corrections), F-008 (WhatsApp/email traceability), F-009 (truck orders workflow), F-010/F-022/F-024/F-025 (email/phone multi-channel), F-012 (email-driven changes), F-013 (duplicate EDI deletion via rules), F-015 (Digital Hub intake automation), F-016 (email/spreadsheet India DC), F-018 (MB5T scheduling), F-019 (fax/website intake), F-020 (CSV watcher), F-030 (EDI failure triage), F-031 (amendment workflow), F-033 (Digital Hub gateway), F-035 (Vector reconciliation).
  - KPIs:
    - Reduction in manual re-keying (F-029 pattern replicated in B items; specific items F-003, F-022).
    - Lower transcription error rates (F-022, F-024).
    - Fewer EDI failure escalations (F-030) and fewer duplicates (F-013).
    - Increased traceability of WhatsApp/email communications (F-008).
  - Exit Criteria:
    - Shadow performance meets path decision confidence rationale per item (e.g., dedup rules stability in F-013 at noted confidence).
    - CSR approval workflow stable; audit trail captures evidence provenance (supports F-008, F-036).
  - Governance:
    - Side-Car configurations reviewed regionally; all posting via standard ERP interfaces.
    - Data privacy handling for WhatsApp/email artifacts governed by local policy; evidence references kept in Side-Car only.

- Wave 3 — Advanced Reasoning (Path C)
  - Scope: F-029 (multi-channel unstructured intake), F-034 (Power Street adaptive normalization), F-036 (dispute/deduction triage).
  - KPIs:
    - Precision/recall of OCR/NLP for PDFs/faxes/emails (F-029, F-019).
    - Successful normalization of mobile payloads with reduced same-day delays (F-034).
    - Mean time to resolution for disputes with cross-system evidence reconciliation (F-036).
  - Exit Criteria:
    - Co-Pilot adoption for structured recommendations; Autopilot only when agent outputs consistently align with path confidence rationales and regional sign-off.
    - No ERP kernel changes; all advanced reasoning contained in Side-Car.
  - Governance:
    - Model monitoring for perception services; human-in-the-loop required during Shadow/Co-Pilot.
    - Escalation paths defined to CSR operations when confidence falls below path decision expectations.

- Program-Level Governance
  - Clean Core Guardrail: Prohibit kernel modifications; run all custom logic in Side-Car; use standard ERP interfaces (supported by [DOC1]/[DOC2] showing transaction and EDI-based operations).
  - Evidence Traceability: All decisions link to friction IDs and [DOC1]/[DOC2] references; Side-Car maintains evidence registry per order.
  - Risk Controls: Exception volumes monitored; rollback plans retain manual process where needed during Shadow.

## 12. Appendix: Control and Operability Baseline — Schema validation, retries, idempotency, governance

- Schema Validation
  - Enforce Side-Car intake schema for headers/lines/delivery/evidence. Detect missing or malformed fields common in manual channels (aligns with F-029 issues).
  - Validate incoterms and export fields pre-posting (F-002).

- Idempotency and Deduplication
  - Maintain order source fingerprints to avoid duplicate postings (addresses F-013 duplicate EDI orders).
  - Implement hash-based matching for repeated emails/CSVs (F-020).

- Retries and Backoff
  - Resilient handling for EDI/IDoc posting failures (F-030) with controlled retries and alerting.
  - CSV watcher re-ingestion when shared folder pickup fails (F-020), with monitoring hooks.

- Exception Management
  - Define workflows for exceptions/new SKUs/data sync failures (F-001), with deterministic resolution catalogs.
  - Digital Hub gateway errors for China (F-033) captured and routed through Side-Car queues.

- Availability and Pre-Check Controls
  - Automate MB5T-like checks scheduling (India, F-018) in Side-Car, logging outcomes before ERP availability actions; preserve Clean Core by avoiding kernel code.

- Traceability and Audit
  - Archive WhatsApp/email correspondence to Side-Car evidence ledger linked to orders (F-008).
  - Retain fax/PDF originals and OCR confidence scores to support manual QA in Shadow (F-019, F-029).

- Security and Compliance
  - Respect regional gateways and policies per registry JSON (China Digital Hub true, etc.).
  - PII handling for communications follows local data protection; evidence references stored, not content in ERP.

- Operability Dashboards
  - Channel-level throughput and error metrics (aligning to friction “Delays and rework” across items).
  - EDI failure heatmaps (F-030), duplicate occurrence trends (F-013), and acknowledgement SLA adherence (F-004, F-017, F-023).

- Clean Core Governance
  - Change approvals ensure Side-Car-only customization; ERP remains System of Record with standard transactions (VA01/VA02/VA03, [DOC1]/[DOC2]).
  - Periodic audits verify no kernel exits or user-exit modifications beyond standard configuration.

## 13. Executive Simplified Summary

Deploy an Order Intake Side-Car that absorbs all custom logic and automates both deterministic rules and agentic perception while keeping ERP Clean Core. Start in Shadow mode to mirror manual workflows and prove accuracy across the documented friction points and regional channels. Move to Co-Pilot and Autopilot in waves aligned to Path A/B/C classifications once confidence matches the path decision rationale.

## Executive Summary

_Section pending LLM elaboration._

## Cognitive Friction Analysis

_Section pending LLM elaboration._

## Architecture of the Future State

_Section pending LLM elaboration._

## Technical Stack

**Clean Core enforcement is explicit: all custom logic is isolated in the Side-Car layer and never embedded in the ERP kernel.**


_Section pending LLM elaboration._

## The Trust Gap Protocol

_Section pending LLM elaboration._

## Executive Simplified Summary

This design gives McCain one intelligent intake layer that reduces manual work and speeds order processing across regions. By keeping custom logic in the Side-Car and using only standard ERP APIs, the solution protects SAP stability and lowers upgrade risk. The trust-gated rollout lets the business scale automation safely while improving accuracy, cycle time, and service quality.

pain_points: Manual multi-channel order intake (email/PDF/fax/spreadsheet) is re-keyed into SAP. (Refs: DOC1, DOC2)
Order capture failures require manual triage for EDI/data-quality exceptions. (Refs: DOC1)
Order changes are processed manually in SAP (VA02/VA03) after initial intake. (Refs: DOC1, DOC2)
Order-type branching (standard vs consignment) is resolved manually during entry. (Refs: DOC1, DOC2)
China intake must pass through Digital Hub before SAP posting. (Refs: DOC1)
Uruguay Power Street truck-loading orders require adaptive normalization before ERP posting. (Refs: DOC1)
South Africa indirect orders need next-day Vector backward integration reconciliation. (Refs: DOC1)
Deduction and dispute triage requires manual evidence reconciliation across systems. (Refs: DOC1)

McCain    4|Signavio GmbH

1.3 Elements
Process elements
01.05.01.01 Receive Customer Order Request

Organization units:  01.05.01.03 Enter Order Into System - Customer Solutions/3PL

Organization units:  01.05.01.03 Enter Order Into System - Customer Solutions/3PL

Exclusive (XOR) Gateway
Organization units:  01.05.01.03 Enter Order Into System - Customer Solutions/3PL
Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP
Task
NORTH AMERICA (NA)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), Esker, CSV (OpenText), EDI
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker) / Manual (exceptions, new SKUs, data sync
failures)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order with order number generated in SAP
Process Note:
• "EDI & Esker are dominant for order intake (Canada & US domestic orders)"
• "OpenText is heavily used for automated entry (Export orders -- JKSEAT region)"
• "Manual entry and intervention are required for exceptions, new SKUs, or data sync
failures"
• "For export manual changes are required on specific scenarios ex. Same sold to/ship to and
different incoterms depending on loading WH, when samples are loading along with a full
container load"
• "CSV file transfers are unique to NA, used by Canada domestic for some samples and by
JKSEAT region for customer orders"
Key Comment: "Manual entry and intervention are required for exceptions, new SKUs, or data
sync failures. For export manual changes are required on specific scenarios ex. Same sold to/
ship to and different incoterms depending on loading WH, when samples are loading along with
a full container load."
Unique Aspect: CSV file processing unique to North America; OpenText boundary system
for export orders; Frequent manual review due to diversity of intake channels and customer
changes
SAP Migration Consideration: VA01 usage already established; Need to ensure Esker and
OpenText integration validated in S/4HANA; CSV file handling process must be maintained
 
MEXICO
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service, QSR, Retail), SAP VA01 (Assumption), Edicom/EDI
(Retail), Gmail (Food service)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Food service via Thalamus) / Automated (EDI for retail)
McCain    5|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System order generated by platform (Thalamus/EDI/SAP)
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is the main platform for food service and QSR; Edicom EDI is used for retail"
• "Manual entry is common, especially for food service; EDI is automated for retail"
• "Heavy reliance on Gmail for food service notifications and customer communication"
• "Order acknowledgment can be automated or manual, and may be requested by the
customer"
• "Common check points: price, route, warehouse, weight, duplicate order, requested delivery
date, etc."
Key Comment: "Heavy reliance on Gmail for food service notifications and customer
communication"
Unique Aspect: Gmail extensively used for food service operational communications;
Thalamus serves multiple channels (Food service, QSR, Retail); Edicom EDI specific to retail
channel
SAP Migration Consideration: Thalamus and Edicom EDI integrations must be validated;
Gmail-based workflow formalization needed; VA01 standard transaction confirmed
 
CALATIN (Central America & Caribbean)
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service), SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI (Retail), Day & Ross (DRTN
for NA-sourced)
Manual/Automated: Manual (frequent) / Automated (EDI for retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record in SAP
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is used for food service; SAP for system entry"
• "Manual entry is frequent, especially for exports and smaller segments"
• "Exports require extra documentation and compliance checks"
• "NA-sourced orders may integrate with Day & Ross (DRTN)"
• "Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with full traceability for export
compliance"
Key Comment: "Exports require extra documentation and compliance checks" and "NA-sourced
orders may integrate with Day & Ross (DRTN)"
Unique Aspect: Export-focused operations with enhanced documentation requirements; Day
& Ross (DRTN) integration for NA-sourced orders; Thalamus for food service segment
SAP Migration Consideration: Export documentation workflows must be preserved; DRTN
integration for NA-sourced orders needs validation; Manual export handling processes require
clear procedures in S/4HANA
 
ANDEAN (Colombia)
System/Tool: Thalamus, Carvajal, SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI
Manual/Automated: Manual (portal handling) / Automated (EDI/Carvajal for retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record
Process Note:
• "Thalamus and Carvajal are primary intake platforms; SAP is used for system entry"
McCain    6|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Manual portal handling, especially for food service (Actualmente se esta migrando a
Thalamus)"
• "EDI/Carvajal standards apply for retail and exports"
• "Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with data retention for
compliance"
Key Comment: "Manual portal handling, especially for food service.(Actualmente se esta
migrando a Thalamus)"
Unique Aspect: Carvajal platform specific to Andean region for retail; Migration to Thalamus
in progress; EDI/Carvajal standards for retail and exports
SAP Migration Consideration: Thalamus migration ongoing - ensure smooth transition;
Carvajal integration must be validated in S/4HANA; Data retention for compliance mandatory
 
BRAZIL
System/Tool: SAP Fiori (Primary), SAP ECC VA01 (Exceptions: CPU, McD, BK, maritime,
donations/samples), EDI, Neo Grid (Retail)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Fiori entries) / Automated (EDI, Neo Grid)
Performer: CSR (Fiori) / SALES ADM Team (SAP ECC exceptions)
Output: SAP order generated in Fiori or ECC depending on scenario
Process Note:
• "SAP Fiori is the main portal for domestic and CPU orders; SAP ECC VA01 is used for special
cases (CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples)"
• "SALES ADM team manages SAP ECC entries for exceptions and special orders"
• "WhatsApp/email are common for customer communication and error resolution"
• "Proforma invoices for CPU orders are issued via SAP ECC VF01/VF02"
• "Strong SAP Fiori adoption; exceptions handled manually in ECC"
Key Comment: "SAP Fiori is the main portal for domestic and CPU orders; SAP ECC VA01 is used
for special cases (CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples)" and "SALES ADM team manages
SAP ECC entries for exceptions and special orders"
Unique Aspect: Dual system architecture (Fiori primary + ECC exceptions); SALES ADM team
handles special scenarios; Strong Fiori adoption among all LATAM regions; WhatsApp business
communication; Proforma invoice generation (VF01/VF02)
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Dual system (Fiori + ECC) needs consolidation
strategy in S/4HANA; Special handling for CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples must be
accommodated; SALES ADM team workflows require clear definition; Proforma invoice process
(VF01/VF02) validation
 
URUGUAY
System/Tool: Thalamus, Power Street, EDI, SAP VA01 (Assumption)
Manual/Automated: Manual (domestic via Thalamus/Power Street) / Automated (EDI retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record
Process Note:
• "EDI, Thalamus, and Power Street are used for order intake; Power Street is unique to
Uruguay"
• "Manual entry is common for domestic orders; EDI for retail"
• "WhatsApp and email are standard for customer communication and error resolution"
McCain    7|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Power Street: Tool to input customer orders for direct and indirect sales. This is used to
load orders on every truck in Uruguay. In this country, sales is a little different from the rest
of McCain. Orders are loaded on the spot (trucks)."
Key Comment: "Power Street is unique to Uruguay" and "This is used to load orders on every
truck in Uruguay. In this country, sales is a little different from the rest of McCain. Orders are
loaded on the spot (trucks)."
Unique Aspect: Power Street ONLY exists in Uruguay; On-the-spot truck loading model;
Unique sales operational model different from rest of McCain; Direct and indirect sales via
mobile tool
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Power Street unique to Uruguay must be integrated
or replaced in S/4HANA; Mobile truck loading workflow is business-critical; Operational model
significantly different from other regions
 
ARGENTINA
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service), Krikos360/EDI (Retail), Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone (QSR),
SAP VA01 (Assumption)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Food service, QSR) / Automated (EDI retail via Krikos360)
Performer: CSR / Operations (varies by channel)
Output: System order by platform (Thalamus/Krikos360/SAP)
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is used for food service; Krikos360/EDI for retail; Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone for
QSR"
• "Manual entry is standard for food service and QSR; EDI for retail"
• "WhatsApp and Gmail are common for rapid customer communication"
• "Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy; EDI for retail"
Key Comment: "WhatsApp and Gmail are common for rapid customer communication" and
"Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy"
Unique Aspect: Krikos360 platform (Planexware) specific to Argentina retail; Multi-channel
digital communication (Gmail, WhatsApp, Phone) for QSR; Channel-specific tool strategy
SAP Migration Consideration: Krikos360/Planexware integration validation critical; WhatsApp/
Gmail workflow formalization needed; Multi-channel tool landscape requires consolidation
assessment
 
APAC-ANZ (Australia & New Zealand)
System/Tool: Portalink, EDI, Email, SAP VA02/VA03 (NOT VA01 for EDI)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Portalink) / Manual (email-based changes)
Performer: Customer Solutions Team
Output: SAP sales order
Process Note:
• "EDI orders are handled automatically in SAP . All changes to customer orders, regardless of
how the original order was received, are processed manually in SAP based on email"
• "Portalink Integration: Serves as critical boundary system converting non-EDI orders into
SAP-compatible format, enabling immediate automated acknowledgements for non-EDI
customers"
• "ANZ: VA01 is not used for EDI order entry. VA02 and VA03 is used"
• "HighRadius Financial Integration: Used for credit checks and payment tracking"
• "Excel Macro Automation: End-of-day order confirmations automated through Excel
macros"
McCain    8|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Z7 CSR Hold Implementation: Universal application of Z7 CSR hold enables manual review
and allocation rule enforcement by commercial team"
Key Comment: "EDI orders are handled automatically in SAP . All changes to customer orders,
regardless of how the original order was received, are processed manually in SAP based on
email" and "VA01 is not used for EDI order entry. VA02 and VA03 is used"
Unique Aspect: CRITICAL: VA01 NOT used for EDI order entry - ANZ uses VA02/VA03;
Portalink boundary system converts non-EDI to SAP format; HighRadius for credit/payment;
Excel macro for end-of-day confirmations; Z7 CSR Hold universal; Duplicate EDI order manual
deletion required
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - ANZ does NOT use VA01 for EDI orders (uses VA02/
VA03) - This is UNIQUE and must be carefully evaluated in S/4HANA migration; Portalink
boundary system integration critical; HighRadius integration validation; Excel macro process
may need modernization; Z7 CSR Hold implementation; ATP functionality gap (currently no ATP)
 
APAC-CHINA
System/Tool: Digital Hub (Cloud-based portal - MANDATORY for ALL orders), SAP VA01 (via
EDI integration from Digital Hub)
Manual/Automated: Manual entry into Digital Hub / Automated (Digital Hub to SAP via EDI)
Performer: Customer Service Team
Output: SAP sales order triggered via EDI from Digital Hub after validation
Process Note:
• "All customer purchase orders (retail or food service channels) are received and processed
through the cloud-based Digital Hub portal"
• "Orders are manually entered into the Digital Hub, where validations and changes are
performed before triggering sales orders into SAP via EDI integration"
• "No Direct Customer EDI: All orders flow through Digital Hub regardless of customer
technical capability"
• "Real-time Integration: Direct integration with warehouse management systems provides
accurate, current inventory data for ATP decisions"
• "Auto-cancellation Logic: Sophisticated cancellation workflows with reason codes (Y1, Y5) for
unpaid orders enable automated revenue protection"
• "ZF delivery blocks preferred over credit blocks for payment terms enforcement"
Key Comment: "All customer purchase orders (retail or food service channels) are received and
processed through the cloud-based Digital Hub portal" and "Orders are manually entered into
the Digital Hub, where validations and changes are performed before triggering sales orders
into SAP via EDI integration"
Unique Aspect: Digital Hub MANDATORY for 100% of orders regardless of channel; No
direct customer-to-SAP EDI; Dual ATP (Digital Hub "light" + SAP "final"); ZF delivery block for
prepayment instead of credit blocks; Auto-cancel Y5 rejection code for unpaid prepay orders;
WMS real-time integration; Channel-specific CRM (Food Service) vs email (Retail/QSR)
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Digital Hub is mandatory gateway (no direct SAP
access); Digital Hub to S/4HANA EDI integration must be validated; Dual ATP mechanism must
be maintained; ZF delivery block logic; Auto-cancellation workflows (Y1, Y5); WMS integration
validation; Prepayment enforcement logic
 
APAC-INDIA
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI via OpenText (Reliance ONLY), Email, Spreadsheet,
Bheem DMS
McCain    9|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI for select modern trade) / Manual (DC staff entry for non-
EDI)
Performer: DC Executives / Customer Service Team
Output: SAP sales order
Process Note:
• "EDI orders from select customers in modern trade (e.g.,: Reliance) are automatically
integrated into SAP via OpenText, while non-EDI orders received by email or spreadsheet are
manually entered by distribution center staff"
• "DC-based Processing: Decentralized order entry model contrasts with other regions'
centralized approaches"
• "Minimal Automation: Limited automated order acknowledgements (only Reliance) reflects
low digitalization maturity"
• "Reliance Special Treatment: Only customer receiving automated order acknowledgements,
indicating strategic partnership requirements"
• "Manual-first Philosophy: Preference for manual processes over automation, possibly due
to complexity and control requirements"
• "MB5T used for stock in transit checks, crucial for multi-location inventory management"
Key Comment: "EDI orders from select customers in modern trade (e.g.,: Reliance) are
automatically integrated into SAP via OpenText, while non-EDI orders received by email or
spreadsheet are manually entered by distribution center staff" and "Only customer receiving
automated order acknowledgements (Reliance)"
Unique Aspect: Decentralized DC-based order entry model; ONLY Reliance has EDI and
automated acknowledgement; Manual-first philosophy; OpenText for select modern
trade EDI; Export orders processed in monthly cycles; MB5T for stock in transit checks
(multi-location); Bheem DMS for retail claims; 100% credit block by default; FSSAI license
validation at order creation; TDP days (10/20/30) affect transport capacity
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Decentralized DC-based entry model unique to India;
OpenText integration for Reliance EDI; Manual entry by DC staff workflows; Minimal automation
- high manual dependency; MB5T stock in transit functionality; FSSAI license validation must be
maintained; Default credit block process; TDP days operational logic
 
APAC-JKSEAT (Japan, Korea, SE Asia & Taiwan)
System/Tool:
• Japan: SAP VA01/ME21 (Assumption), Fax, Customer websites, EDI, CSV macro
• KSEAT: SAP VA01 (Assumption), CSV files, Macro/EDI watcher, Email
Manual/Automated: Manual (Fax, websites in Japan) / Automated (CSV macro, EDI for both)
Performer: Customer Service Team
Output: SAP sales order, Proforma invoice (as order confirmation)
Process Note:
• Japan: "Customer orders are captured through multiple channels: fax, customer websites,
EDI, and CSV files. Fax and website orders are manually entered into SAP , while EDI and CSV
file orders are automatically integrated into SAP"
• KSEAT: "Orders are received via email as either CSV files or manual requests. CSV files are
uploaded to a shared folder, then picked up by EDI or a macro for automatic entry into SAP ,
while manual orders are entered directly into SAP"
• "Traditional Communication Channels: Japan maintains fax communication alongside
modern channels, reflecting local business culture and customer preferences"
McCain    10|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Advanced Order Planning: Orders placed 6 weeks to 4 months in advance eliminates need
for real-time inventory visibility but requires sophisticated planning"
• "CSV Automation: KSEAT uses shared folder mechanism with macro/EDI pickup for CSV
automation"
• "Proforma Invoice as confirmation: KC sends Proforma Invoice (manual PDF from SAP) as
order confirmation; HAVI/McDonald's excluded"
• "No local ATP/MOQ gating at JKC; orders are taken weeks to months ahead (min ~6 weeks),
and sourcing regions (e.g., NA, EU) run ATP on their side"
Key Comment: "Japan: Customer orders are captured through multiple channels: fax, customer
websites, EDI, and CSV files" and "Orders placed 6 weeks to 4 months in advance" and "No local
ATP/MOQ gating at JKC"
Unique Aspect: Traditional fax still used in Japan; CSV macro/shared folder automation
in KSEAT; Proforma invoice used as order confirmation (not standard acknowledgement);
No local ATP - sourcing regions handle ATP; Long lead times (6 weeks to 4 months); ME21 for
purchase orders alongside VA01; Power BI for shipment tracking; Regional orders 6-16 weeks
ahead of ship date
SAP Migration Consideration: Japan VA01/ME21 usage must be validated; Fax integration/
workflow documentation needed; CSV macro automation from shared folders; Proforma invoice
generation process; No local ATP - sourcing region dependency; Long planning horizon (6
weeks-4 months) must be supported; Power BI integration for tracking
 
GREAT BRITAIN (GB)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption for order creation), EDI, Esker, Email (Manual), ZV05N
(for display)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker) / Manual (email orders)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: Manually entered order (from email) / Automated order from EDI/Esker

landscapes, integrating key business processes and data across departments, and enabling
efficient management of resources, operations, and reporting.Key 
Features of SAP ECC:Centralized database for seamless data flow across business functions

SAP CO, a module within SAP ECC that focuses on internal accounting and management
accounting, providing tools for planning, controlling, and analyzing costs and revenues. It is tightly
integrated with SAP FI (Financial Accounting) and is often referred to as SAP FICO. SAP CO helps
businesses track costs associated with various business processes, allocate costs, and manage
profitability analysis.
