strategy_report_markdown:
# Re-Imagined Strategy Report: Order Intake

## 1. Executive Summary — One Big Move

The One Big Move is to deploy a Clean Core-compliant Order Intake Side-Car as a System of Intelligence that orchestrates multi-channel intake, deterministic rules, and agentic perception across regions, while leaving the ERP kernel unchanged. This directly targets global manual re-keying from email/PDF/fax/spreadsheets (F-029, [DOC1], [DOC2]), EDI failure triage (F-030, [DOC1]), mandatory China Digital Hub handoffs (F-033, [DOC1]), and regional manual holds/acknowledgements (F-014, F-023) by absorbing logic and automation in the Side-Car. In the current Trust Gap Phase: Shadow, the Side-Car runs in parallel to human CSR workflows and SAP transactions (VA01/VA02, per evidence), learning from friction patterns and proving precision against path decisions (A/B/C). This move reduces cycle time and errors documented across regions (e.g., NA, Mexico, EMENA, APAC-ANZ/Japan/India), standardizes gateway decisions (F-032), and ensures all custom logic resides outside the ERP kernel, enforcing Clean Core.

## 2. Cognitive Friction Analysis — The friction table (reproduce as-is)

| Friction_ID | Current_Manual_Action | Where_in_Process | Trigger_or_Input_Channel | Region_Impacted | Systems_or_Tools_Mentioned | Why_It's_Friction | Evidence | Open_Questions |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| F-001 | CSR manually keys/fixes orders for exceptions, new SKUs, and data sync failures | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Exceptions/new SKUs/data sync failures (stated) | NORTH AMERICA (NA) | SAP VA01 (Assumption), Esker, CSV (OpenText), EDI | Delays and rework to resolve failed/exception cases; increased error risk | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements > Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP (NA): “Manual entry and intervention are required for exceptions, new SKUs, or data sync failures” | What % of NA orders require manual intervention? |
| F-002 | CSR performs manual changes on export orders (e.g., Incoterms adjustments, sample-with-FCL scenarios) | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Export scenarios with same sold-to/ship-to, varying Incoterms; samples with full container | NORTH AMERICA (NA) | SAP VA01 (Assumption) | Manual edits add cycle time and increase compliance/error risk for export orders | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (NA): “For export manual changes are required on specific scenarios ex. Same sold to/ship to and different incoterms… when samples are loading along with a full container load” | Which fields are changed manually and how often? |
| F-003 | CSR manually enters food service orders; relies on Gmail for notifications/communications | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Thalamus (food service), Gmail notifications | MEXICO | Thalamus, SAP VA01 (Assumption), Edicom/EDI (Retail), Gmail | Email dependency and manual entry create delays and transcription errors | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (MEXICO): “Manual entry is common, especially for food service… Heavy reliance on Gmail for food service notifications and customer communication” | Is there a standard template for Gmail orders to reduce errors? |
| F-004 | Manual order acknowledgements sent upon customer request | Send Order Acknowledgement | Customer request (stated) | MEXICO | Gmail (stated), SAP VA01 (Assumption) | Extra manual step adds workload and latency; inconsistent customer experience | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (MEXICO): “Order acknowledgment can be automated or manual, and may be requested by the customer” | Through which channel/system are manual acknowledgements sent? |
| F-005 | CSR manually enters export/smaller-segment orders; performs manual corrections/changes with traceability | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Exports; smaller segments; change requests | CALATIN (Central America & Caribbean) | Thalamus, SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI, Day & Ross (DRTN) | Manual effort for exports/compliance drives delay and rework | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (CALATIN): “Manual entry is frequent, especially for exports and smaller segments… Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with full traceability for export compliance” | What specific export documents/fields require manual handling? |
| F-006 | CSR handles food service orders manually via portals; manual corrections/changes for compliance | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Food service portal handling | ANDEAN (Colombia) | Thalamus, Carvajal, SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI | Portal-driven manual handling increases processing time and error risk | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (ANDEAN): “Manual portal handling, especially for food service… Corrections and change requests are handled manually” | Volume split between manual portal vs EDI? |
| F-007 | SALES ADM team manually enters exceptions/special orders in ECC instead of Fiori | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Special cases (CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples) | BRAZIL | SAP Fiori, SAP ECC VA01 | Dual-system handoffs create delays, training overhead, and error potential | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (BRAZIL): “SAP Fiori is the main portal… SAP ECC VA01 is used for special cases… SALES ADM team manages SAP ECC entries for exceptions and special orders” | What is the volume of exceptions routed to ECC? |
| F-008 | CSR/Sales resolve issues and coordinate via WhatsApp/email | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | WhatsApp/email communications | BRAZIL | WhatsApp, Email, SAP Fiori/ECC | Informal channels hinder traceability and standardization; risk of missed info | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (BRAZIL): “WhatsApp/email are common for customer communication and error resolution” | Are conversations archived to the order record? |
| F-009 | CSR manually inputs domestic orders; on-the-spot truck orders entered via Power Street | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | On-the-spot truck loading | URUGUAY | Power Street, Thalamus, EDI, SAP VA01 (Assumption) | Real-time manual entry under time pressure increases error risk and variability | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (URUGUAY): “Manual entry is common for domestic orders… Power Street… used to load orders on every truck… Orders are loaded on the spot (trucks).” | How are Power Street entries synchronized with SAP to avoid discrepancies? |
| F-010 | CSR/Operations manually enter Food service and QSR orders from Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Gmail, WhatsApp, Phone (QSR); Thalamus (Food service) | ARGENTINA | Thalamus, Krikos360/EDI, Gmail, WhatsApp, Phone, SAP VA01 (Assumption) | Multichannel manual intake creates transcription errors and latency | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (ARGENTINA): “Manual entry is standard for food service and QSR… Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone for QSR” | Are there structured templates for QSR orders? |
| F-011 | Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy orders | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Retail/hardcopy | ARGENTINA | Krikos360/EDI (Retail), SAP VA01 (Assumption) | Extra manual QA adds cycle time and rework | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (ARGENTINA): “Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy” | What are the common error types caught in manual review? |
| F-012 | CS team manually processes all customer order changes in SAP based on email | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP (changes via VA02) | Email-driven change requests | APAC-ANZ (Australia & New Zealand) | SAP VA02/VA03, EDI, Portalink, Email | Manual rework for every change causes delays and error risk | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (APAC-ANZ): “All changes to customer orders… are processed manually in SAP based on email” | What % of orders receive change requests? |
| F-013 | Manual deletion of duplicate EDI orders | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Duplicate EDI order occurrence | APAC-ANZ (Australia & New Zealand) | SAP (VA02/VA03 noted), EDI | Manual cleanup is time-consuming; risk if duplicates are missed | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (APAC-ANZ) Unique Aspect: “Duplicate EDI order manual deletion required” | Frequency and root causes of duplicate EDI orders? |
| F-014 | Manual review due to universal Z7 CSR hold before allocation | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Z7 CSR hold applied universally | APAC-ANZ (Australia & New Zealand) | SAP (Z7 CSR hold) | Intentional hold forces manual review, adding processing time | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (APAC-ANZ): “Z7 CSR Hold Implementation: Universal application… enables manual review and allocation rule enforcement” | Criteria for releasing holds and average time-to-release? |
| F-015 | CS team manually enters 100% of orders into Digital Hub portal before SAP integration | Enter Order Into System | All orders (Retail and Food service) via Digital Hub | APAC-CHINA | Digital Hub, SAP VA01 (via EDI) | Full manual portal entry for every order increases cycle time and error exposure | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (APAC-CHINA): “Orders are manually entered into the Digital Hub… All customer purchase orders… are received and processed through the… Digital Hub portal” | Average handling time per order in Digital Hub? |
| F-016 | DC executives manually key non-EDI orders from email/spreadsheets into SAP | Enter Order Into System | Email, Spreadsheet (non-EDI orders) | APAC-INDIA | SAP VA01 (Assumption), OpenText (EDI Reliance), Email, Spreadsheet | Decentralized manual entry increases variability, errors, and delays | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (APAC-INDIA): “non-EDI orders received by email or spreadsheet are manually entered by distribution center staff” | Are there SOPs or templates for DC data entry? |
| F-017 | Manual order acknowledgements for non-Reliance customers (limited automation) | Send Order Acknowledgement | Not specified | APAC-INDIA | OpenText (EDI for Reliance), SAP VA01 (Assumption) | Manual acknowledgements add workload; inconsistent confirmation timing | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (APAC-INDIA): “Minimal Automation: Limited automated order acknowledgements (only Reliance)” | Which channel is used for manual acknowledgements (email/SAP output)? |
| F-018 | Staff manually runs stock-in-transit checks (MB5T) for multi-location inventory | 01.05.02.01 Check Product and Service Availability | Multi-location inventory scenarios | APAC-INDIA | SAP MB5T | Manual checks extend cycle time; risk of outdated/incorrect decisions | L4 - Enter Order Into System, 1.3 Elements (APAC-INDIA): “MB5T used for stock in transit checks, crucial for multi-location inventory management” | Can MB5T checks be automated or embedded in ATP? |

## 4. Architecture of the Future State — Hub-and-spoke operating model with agent personas

- Operating Model
  - Hub: Order Intake Side-Car (System of Intelligence)
    - Purpose: Centralize intake, classification, validation, and orchestration for multi-channel orders while preserving ERP Clean Core. Anchored by evidence of global manual intake across email/PDF/fax/spreadsheets (F-029, [DOC1], [DOC2]) and region-specific manual patterns (F-003 Mexico Gmail; F-008 Brazil WhatsApp; F-019 Japan fax/web; F-022 GB email; F-024 EMENA email/phone).
    - Scope: Intake adapters, perception (OCR/NLP) for unstructured channels, deterministic rule services for export/incoterms and holds, exception triage for EDI failures (F-030, [DOC1]), gateway compliance (F-033 Digital Hub).
    - Clean Core Enforcement: All custom logic resides in the Side-Car. The ERP kernel is not modified; interactions are via standard APIs/IDoc interfaces referenced in [DOC1] (EDI/IDoc failure mentions) and SAP transactions (VA01/VA02/VA03) evidenced in multiple friction items.
  - Spokes: Regional channel connectors
    - NA: EDI/Esker/CSV adapters to reduce manual exceptions (F-001).
    - Mexico: Gmail adapter for food service notifications (F-003), acknowledgement workflow (F-004).
    - CALATIN/ANDEAN: Portal adapters (Thalamus, Carvajal) for food service and export corrections (F-005, F-006).
    - Brazil: WhatsApp and email connectors (F-008), Fiori/ECC routing rationalization for special cases (F-007).
    - Uruguay: Power Street mobile intake normalization (F-009, F-034).
    - Argentina: Multichannel intake (Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone, Krikos360/EDI) (F-010, F-011).
    - APAC-ANZ: EDI monitoring, manual change capture, duplicate detection (F-012, F-013, F-014).
    - APAC-China: Digital Hub integration (F-015, F-033).
    - APAC-India: Email/spreadsheet entry normalization (F-016), acknowledgement workflow (F-017), MB5T check scheduling (F-018).
    - APAC-Japan/KSEAT: Fax OCR, website scraping, CSV watcher (F-019, F-020), proforma generation routing (F-021).
    - GB: Email/Esker/EDI intake (F-022), acknowledgement automation (F-023).
    - EMENA: Email/phone intake reduction (F-024).
    - South Africa: Email direct network intake; Vector EDI recon (F-025, F-035).

- Agent Personas (embedded in Side-Car, not ERP)
  - Intake Scribe: Perceives and transcribes multi-channel orders into structured payloads (supports F-003, F-008, F-010, F-019, F-022, F-024, F-025, F-029).
  - Intent Analyzer: Classifies order-type (standard vs consignment) and determines required rule-based adjustments (supports F-002, F-032, Z7 hold context in F-014).
  - Dispute Judge: Reconciles deductions/disputes across evidence systems (supports F-036).

- Governance and Control Points
  - Decision Gate Standardization: Remove inconsistent manual branching (F-028) by encoding gateway criteria in Side-Car, executed before posting to ERP (Clean Core).
  - Exception Handling: EDI and CSV exceptions processed in Side-Car (F-030, F-020) with alerting and retry, reducing manual triage.
  - Regional Gateway Compliance: Respect China Digital Hub mandate (F-033) and local nuances (regional JSON registry below) while centralizing orchestration.

## 5. Technical Stack — System of Intelligence, System of Record, integration contracts

- System of Intelligence (Side-Car)
  - Channel Adapters: Email (Gmail), WhatsApp, Fax OCR, PDF parsing, CSV watcher for shared folders (KSEAT, F-020), portal connectors (Thalamus, Carvajal), mobile Power Street adapter (Uruguay, F-009/F-034).
  - Perception Services: OCR/NLP for unstructured sources (aligned with F-029, F-019, F-022, F-024).
  - Rule Services: Export/incoterms enforcement (F-002), Z7 hold release criteria (F-014), order-type classification (F-032), deduplication for EDI (F-013), Digital Hub pass-through logic (F-033), acknowledgement workflows (F-004, F-017, F-023).
  - Exception Orchestrator: EDI failure triage (IDoc failure evidence in [DOC1], F-030), CSV pickup failure monitoring (F-020), vector reconciliation scheduling (F-035).
  - Audit and Traceability: Conversation capture from WhatsApp/email (F-008), evidence ledger for disputes (F-036). All maintained in Side-Car, linked to ERP order references.

- System of Record
  - ERP: SAP ECC/ERP (VA01/VA02/VA03 referenced across [DOC1]/[DOC2]).
  - Integration Contracts: Standard IDoc-based EDI processing (referenced in [DOC1] via “EDI failure” and IDoc context), outbound acknowledgements via ERP outputs (F-004, F-017, F-023), no ERP kernel modifications. All posting/changes executed through published APIs/IDoc interfaces to maintain Clean Core.

- Integration Principles (Clean Core)
  - No custom code in ERP kernel. Side-Car performs all custom logic, sends validated, fully classified payloads to ERP.
  - ERP remains authoritative for order record; Side-Car maintains intake provenance, evidence links, and channel artifacts.

## 6. Integration Design Deep Dive — Staged processing contract (intake, classification, ERP posting)

- Stage 1: Intake
  - Trigger Channels: Email, PDF, Fax, Spreadsheet (global, F-029); WhatsApp (Brazil, Argentina, F-008/F-010); Portals (Thalamus/Carvajal/Krikos360, F-003/F-006/F-011/F-010); CSV shared folder (KSEAT, F-020); Mobile (Power Street, Uruguay, F-009/F-034); Digital Hub (China, F-015/F-033).
  - Contract: Side-Car standard schema: Header (sold-to/ship-to, incoterms), Line Items (SKU, quantity, UoM), Delivery (date/time windows), References (PO, portal IDs), Evidence (channel message IDs, file hashes).
  - Controls: Schema validation; channel provenance capture (for F-008 traceability); duplicate detection (F-013).

- Stage 2: Classification
  - Order-Type Determination: Standard vs consignment per documented gateways (F-032, [DOC1], [DOC2]).
  - Rule-Based Adjustments: Export adjustments (incoterms, sample-with-FCL scenarios) per North America evidence (F-002); Z7 CSR hold treatment (ANZ, F-014); China mandatory Digital Hub step enforcement (F-033).
  - Exception Prediction: Identify EDI risk (F-030) and portal anomalies, route to agentic review (Intake Scribe/Intent Analyzer) during Shadow phase for human comparison.

- Stage 3: ERP Posting
  - Posting Path: Side-Car converts classified payload to ERP-compatible structure and submits via standard interfaces (evidence of EDI/IDoc context in [DOC1]).
  - Acknowledgement: Automated confirmations for regions with manual acknowledgements (Mexico, India, GB per F-004, F-017, F-023), respecting local templates and registry policies.
  - Change Management: Side-Car orchestrates VA02-like changes when sourced from email (F-012, [DOC1], [DOC2]). During Shadow, changes are suggested, with CSR finalization.
  - Reconciliation: Vector backward integration schedule (SA, F-035), MB5T check automation for India (F-018) as pre/posting availability checks coordinated outside ERP (Clean Core).

- Operational Safeguards
  - Idempotency: Prevent duplicate ERP postings (mitigate F-013).
  - Retry/Backoff: EDI/IDoc transient failures (F-030).
  - Observability: Channel-level and region-level dashboards for intake volume and exception rates, anchored to friction points.

## 7. Agent Persona Reasoning Model — Intake Scribe, Intent Analyzer, Dispute Judge boundaries

- Intake Scribe
  - Mission: Transform unstructured inputs into structured order payloads with evidence markers. Applies to email, WhatsApp, fax, phone summaries, PDFs, and spreadsheets (F-003, F-008, F-010, F-019, F-022, F-024, F-025, F-029).
  - Boundaries: Does not commit orders in ERP; prepares payloads and confidence scores. During Shadow, runs parallel to CSR manual entry; discrepancies are flagged for review.
  - Evidence Anchors: Unstructured intake documented across regions and channels ([DOC1], [DOC2], F-029; region-specific items above).

- Intent Analyzer
  - Mission: Decide order-type (standard vs consignment) and apply rule services for exports/incoterms, consignment routing, Z7 hold logic (F-002, F-032, F-014).
  - Boundaries: Executes deterministic rules in Side-Car; no kernel logic changes. During Shadow, recommendations rendered to CSR with traceability to evidence references and regional policies.

- Dispute Judge
  - Mission: Triage deductions/disputes by correlating evidence across systems (F-036, [DOC1]).
  - Boundaries: Does not alter ERP financial records; compiles reconciliation packets and recommended actions. Runs in Side-Car; ensures audit trail of evidence provenance.

- Persona Governance
  - Confidence Calibration: Leverage path decision confidence notes (e.g., 90–97%) as initial calibration references; all agent outputs in Shadow require human confirmation.
  - Escalation: When agent confidence is below path-design expectations or channel anomalies exist (e.g., CSV pickup failure per F-020), route to human CSR.

## 8. The Trust Gap Protocol — Shadow / Co-Pilot / Autopilot phases with confidence thresholds

- Shadow (Current Phase; per Process Context)
  - Description: Agents operate in parallel to human workflows; produce payloads and recommendations without committing ERP transactions. CSRs compare Side-Car outputs against their manual entries (supported by evidence of manual entries across regions, e.g., VA01/VA02/VA03 use in [DOC1]/[DOC2]).
  - Confidence Handling: Align to path decision confidences noted for each friction item (e.g., 97% for deduplication F-013 and email intake F-022 classification rationale). Agent outputs are logged with confidence and rationale; any discrepancies trigger remediation and learning.

- Co-Pilot
  - Description: Agents pre-fill ERP operation requests and propose decisions (order-type, holds, acknowledgements) while CSRs approve/override. Anchored to deterministic A-path items (e.g., F-002, F-014, F-032) and B-path items requiring perception but stable patterns (e.g., email intake segments F-022).
  - Confidence Handling: Progression from Shadow to Co-Pilot per friction pathway when observed accuracy aligns with path confidence rationales. Governance boards review empirical precision against friction evidence.

- Autopilot
  - Description: Agents commit transactions via Side-Car→ERP integration for scoped pathways, with post-commit alerts. Applies first to fully deterministic A-path flows (export/incoterms enforcement, acknowledgement automation) and then to B-path segments achieving sustained accuracy and traceability.
  - Confidence Handling: Requires sustained alignment with path decision confidence levels and regional sign-off; exception handling remains in Side-Car.

All phases enforce Clean Core—no ERP kernel changes; Side-Car handles perception, rules, and orchestration.

## 9. Path Design Decisions — Bullet list of each friction point's path assignment with rationale

- F-001 — Path B: Deterministic exception-handling and sync rules can automate these cases; no unstructured inputs require perception or complex reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-002 — Path A: Standard ERP configuration can enforce Incoterms and export rules (rule-based); no need for perception or probabilistic reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-003 — Path B: Requires perception of unstructured Gmail communications and extraction of order details; may need adaptive handling of varied email formats. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-004 — Path B: Order acknowledgements can be sent deterministically via workflow/output rules; no perception or complex reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-005 — Path A: Export and smaller-segment orders can be standardized in core ERP with rule-based fields and compliance checks; no unstructured inputs noted. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-006 — Path B: Portal intake and compliance corrections can be automated with deterministic workflows; no unstructured perception required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-007 — Path A: Enforce standard front-end (Fiori) usage and reduce ECC special cases via core process standardization; rule-based routing, no perception needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-008 — Path B: WhatsApp/email are unstructured channels requiring perception and contextual reasoning to ensure traceability and correct ERP updates. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-009 — Path B: On-the-spot truck orders can be automated via deterministic integration/workflows; no explicit need for perception or complex reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-010 — Path B: Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone inputs are unstructured and require perception and adaptive handling to reduce transcription errors and latency. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-011 — Path B: Retail/hardcopy reviews require perception of unstructured documents and judgment; fits probabilistic reasoning rather than deterministic rules. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-012 — Path B: Email-driven change requests require perception to extract intent and details and may need adaptive handling of varied change scenarios. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-013 — Path B: Duplicate EDI orders can be eliminated via deterministic deduplication rules/IDoc checks; no perception or adaptive reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-014 — Path A: Replace universal manual holds with standard ERP rule-based holds/release criteria; no need for perception or probabilistic logic. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-015 — Path B: Digital Hub intake can be automated with deterministic workflows/forms validation; no perception or complex reasoning indicated. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-016 — Path B: Email/spreadsheet inputs require perception to parse varied formats and reconcile; may need adaptive exception handling by an agent. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-017 — Path B: Order acknowledgements can be auto-generated via deterministic rules; no unstructured perception or complex reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-018 — Path B: Stock-in-transit checks (MB5T) can be scheduled/automated deterministically; no perception or probabilistic reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-019 — Path B: Fax and website orders require perception (OCR/web parsing) and adaptive handling of varied layouts; fits agentic processing. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-020 — Path B: CSV ingestion is structured and can be automated deterministically (watchers/ETL); no perception or adaptive reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-021 — Path A: Use standard ERP output determination to auto-generate/send proforma confirmations (rule-based); no perception/reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-022 — Path B: Email orders require perception of unstructured content and extraction of order data; deterministic rules alone are insufficient. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-023 — Path A: Enable standard ERP auto-notification on order entry (rule-based output); no perception or adaptive reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-024 — Path B: Email and phone inputs are unstructured, requiring perception and adaptive handling to accurately capture orders into ERP. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-025 — Path B: Email-based direct network orders require perception to extract order details; deterministic automation alone is insufficient. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-026 — Path B: Repeated manual touchpoints indicate tasks suitable for deterministic workflow/RPA; no explicit need for perception or probabilistic reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-027 — Path B: Exception branches can be routed via deterministic rules in workflows; adaptive planning not explicitly required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-028 — Path A: Standardize decision gateway criteria in core ERP to eliminate inconsistent manual branching; rule-based configuration suffices. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-029 — Path C: Email/PDF/fax/spreadsheet intake requires perception of unstructured/semi-structured content and adaptive handling of format variance. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-030 — Path B: EDI capture failures can be triaged via deterministic validation/IDoc rules; no perception or probabilistic reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-031 — Path B: Order amendments in SAP can follow deterministic workflows; perception is only needed if sourced from unstructured channels (not specified here). Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-032 — Path A: Order type classification can be enforced via standard ERP rules; deterministic, no perception or adaptive reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-033 — Path B: Mandatory gateway can be streamlined with deterministic integration/workflows; no unstructured perception or probabilistic reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-034 — Path C: Mobile Power Street payloads need adaptive normalization and exception handling; requires perception and dynamic action beyond deterministic rules. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-035 — Path B: Next-day reconciliation can be automated deterministically via integration schedules; no perception or probabilistic reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-036 — Path C: Dispute/deduction triage requires perception across multiple systems/documents and reasoning to reconcile evidence and compliance. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)

## 11. Delivery and Rollout Plan — Wave 1/2/3 with KPIs, exit criteria, and governance

- Guiding Principle: Sequence waves by Path classes and regional readiness evidenced in friction items, while maintaining Clean Core (Side-Car-only custom logic).

- Wave 1 — Deterministic Core (Path A)
  - Scope: F-002 (export/incoterms), F-005 (export/smaller segments standardization), F-007 (Brazil Fiori standardization for exceptions), F-014 (Z7 hold rule-based replacement), F-021 (proforma output determination), F-023 (auto acknowledgement).
  - Regional Touchpoints: NA exports (F-002), CALATIN exports (F-005), Brazil Fiori/ECC rationalization (F-007), ANZ holds (F-014), KSEAT proforma (F-021), GB acknowledgement (F-023).
  - KPIs (derived from friction “Why It’s Friction”):
    - Cycle time reduction for export/edit scenarios (F-002/F-005).
    - Reduction in manual acknowledgements (F-023).
    - Elimination of universal manual holds prior to allocation (F-014).
  - Exit Criteria:
    - Documented rule sets operational in Side-Car; ERP integrations via standard interfaces only (Clean Core).
    - CSR sign-off that manual edits/holds replaced by rule outcomes in Co-Pilot mode.
    - No kernel modifications in ERP.
  - Governance:
    - Region leads validate rules against local practices referenced in [DOC1].
    - Change Advisory Board confirms no core code changes; integration approvals logged.

- Wave 2 — Agentic Perception and Workflow (Path B)
  - Scope: F-001 (exceptions/new SKUs), F-003 (Mexico Gmail intake), F-004/F-017 (acknowledgements workflow automation), F-006 (portal corrections), F-008 (WhatsApp/email traceability), F-009 (truck orders workflow), F-010/F-022/F-024/F-025 (email/phone multi-channel), F-012 (email-driven changes), F-013 (duplicate EDI deletion via rules), F-015 (Digital Hub intake automation), F-016 (email/spreadsheet India DC), F-018 (MB5T scheduling), F-019 (fax/website intake), F-020 (CSV watcher), F-030 (EDI failure triage), F-031 (amendment workflow), F-033 (Digital Hub gateway), F-035 (Vector reconciliation).
  - KPIs:
    - Reduction in manual re-keying (F-029 pattern replicated in B items; specific items F-003, F-022).
    - Lower transcription error rates (F-022, F-024).
    - Fewer EDI failure escalations (F-030) and fewer duplicates (F-013).
    - Increased traceability of WhatsApp/email communications (F-008).
  - Exit Criteria:
    - Shadow performance meets path decision confidence rationale per item (e.g., dedup rules stability in F-013 at noted confidence).
    - CSR approval workflow stable; audit trail captures evidence provenance (supports F-008, F-036).
  - Governance:
    - Side-Car configurations reviewed regionally; all posting via standard ERP interfaces.
    - Data privacy handling for WhatsApp/email artifacts governed by local policy; evidence references kept in Side-Car only.

- Wave 3 — Advanced Reasoning (Path C)
  - Scope: F-029 (multi-channel unstructured intake), F-034 (Power Street adaptive normalization), F-036 (dispute/deduction triage).
  - KPIs:
    - Precision/recall of OCR/NLP for PDFs/faxes/emails (F-029, F-019).
    - Successful normalization of mobile payloads with reduced same-day delays (F-034).
    - Mean time to resolution for disputes with cross-system evidence reconciliation (F-036).
  - Exit Criteria:
    - Co-Pilot adoption for structured recommendations; Autopilot only when agent outputs consistently align with path confidence rationales and regional sign-off.
    - No ERP kernel changes; all advanced reasoning contained in Side-Car.
  - Governance:
    - Model monitoring for perception services; human-in-the-loop required during Shadow/Co-Pilot.
    - Escalation paths defined to CSR operations when confidence falls below path decision expectations.

- Program-Level Governance
  - Clean Core Guardrail: Prohibit kernel modifications; run all custom logic in Side-Car; use standard ERP interfaces (supported by [DOC1]/[DOC2] showing transaction and EDI-based operations).
  - Evidence Traceability: All decisions link to friction IDs and [DOC1]/[DOC2] references; Side-Car maintains evidence registry per order.
  - Risk Controls: Exception volumes monitored; rollback plans retain manual process where needed during Shadow.

## 12. Appendix: Control and Operability Baseline — Schema validation, retries, idempotency, governance

- Schema Validation
  - Enforce Side-Car intake schema for headers/lines/delivery/evidence. Detect missing or malformed fields common in manual channels (aligns with F-029 issues).
  - Validate incoterms and export fields pre-posting (F-002).

- Idempotency and Deduplication
  - Maintain order source fingerprints to avoid duplicate postings (addresses F-013 duplicate EDI orders).
  - Implement hash-based matching for repeated emails/CSVs (F-020).

- Retries and Backoff
  - Resilient handling for EDI/IDoc posting failures (F-030) with controlled retries and alerting.
  - CSV watcher re-ingestion when shared folder pickup fails (F-020), with monitoring hooks.

- Exception Management
  - Define workflows for exceptions/new SKUs/data sync failures (F-001), with deterministic resolution catalogs.
  - Digital Hub gateway errors for China (F-033) captured and routed through Side-Car queues.

- Availability and Pre-Check Controls
  - Automate MB5T-like checks scheduling (India, F-018) in Side-Car, logging outcomes before ERP availability actions; preserve Clean Core by avoiding kernel code.

- Traceability and Audit
  - Archive WhatsApp/email correspondence to Side-Car evidence ledger linked to orders (F-008).
  - Retain fax/PDF originals and OCR confidence scores to support manual QA in Shadow (F-019, F-029).

- Security and Compliance
  - Respect regional gateways and policies per registry JSON (China Digital Hub true, etc.).
  - PII handling for communications follows local data protection; evidence references stored, not content in ERP.

- Operability Dashboards
  - Channel-level throughput and error metrics (aligning to friction “Delays and rework” across items).
  - EDI failure heatmaps (F-030), duplicate occurrence trends (F-013), and acknowledgement SLA adherence (F-004, F-017, F-023).

- Clean Core Governance
  - Change approvals ensure Side-Car-only customization; ERP remains System of Record with standard transactions (VA01/VA02/VA03, [DOC1]/[DOC2]).
  - Periodic audits verify no kernel exits or user-exit modifications beyond standard configuration.

## 13. Executive Simplified Summary

Deploy an Order Intake Side-Car that absorbs all custom logic and automates both deterministic rules and agentic perception while keeping ERP Clean Core. Start in Shadow mode to mirror manual workflows and prove accuracy across the documented friction points and regional channels. Move to Co-Pilot and Autopilot in waves aligned to Path A/B/C classifications once confidence matches the path decision rationale.

    ERP_POST[Post Order]:::core
    ERP_AVAIL[Check Product and Service Availability]:::core
    DB_S4[(Master Data)]:::persistence
  end

  CH_CHAT -->|Webhook| WF_ROUTER

  %% Side-Car orchestration flow
  WF_ROUTER -.->|Normalize Payload Ref DOC1 DOC2| AG_SCRIBE
  WF_ROUTER -.->|Change Queue Ref DOC1 DOC2| WF_CHANGE
  WF_CHANGE -->|Amend Order| AG_INTENT

  DG_ORDER_TYPE -->|Standard| WF_VALIDATOR
  DG_ORDER_TYPE -->|Consignment| WF_VALIDATOR
  WF_VALIDATOR -->|Format| WF_FORMAT
  AG_INTENT -->|Exception to Resolve| AG_DISPUTE
  AG_DISPUTE -.->|Resolve Case| ERP_VA01

  %% Clean Core standard processing
  WF_FORMAT -.->|Post to System| ERP_VA01
  ERP_VA01 -->|Update| ERP_MD
  ERP_MD -.->|Check Data| DB_S4
  ERP_MD ==>|Post to System| ERP_POST

  ERP_POST -->|Availability Sync Ref DOC1| ERP_AVAIL

path_classifier_prompt:
TASK: Path Classification (A/B/C) for Friction Points (Table Output Only)

Role: Principal Enterprise AI Architect and Business Transformation Strategist.
Mode: High reasoning.

Goal
Classify each friction point into exactly one of three paths:
- Path A (Core Standardization): revert/force to standard ERP/CRM functionality.
- Path B (Platform Automation): deterministic automation (workflows/RPA).
- Path C (Agentic AI Deployment): probabilistic reasoning (GenAI agents).

Inputs
You will receive:
1) A "Cognitive Friction Analysis" table of friction points
2) The same source documents (for evidence cross-check)

Suitability Rule (STRICT — must be explicit in justification)
Assign Path C ONLY if the task requires at least one:
- Perception (reading/extracting unstructured inputs like emails/PDF/free text)
- Reasoning (trade-off analysis, multi-step judgment)
- Adaptive Action (dynamic exception handling/planning)
If not, prefer Path B or Path A.

Output (STRICT)
Produce ONLY a JSON array titled "path_classifications".
Each element must have these keys:
- friction_id
- recommended_path (A / B / C)
- suitability_justification (1–2 lines; MUST reference rule-based vs perception/reasoning/adaptive action)
- core_vs_sidecar_orientation (Core / Side-Car / Hybrid)
- human_supervision_needed (Yes / No / Conditional)
- confidence (High / Medium / Low)
- evidence (Document + page/section supporting classification)
- open_questions (if classification depends on missing info, else empty string)

Rules
- Map 1:1 to the Friction_IDs from the friction table.
- Use evidence from the provided documents.
- Do not invent technologies or tools not stated in the documents.
- Keep "Core vs Side-Car" conceptual (no vendor/tool names unless stated).

Begin
Classify all friction points and output the JSON array only.
